title: Privacy Rights - Aadhaar-type Case
description: In 2024, multiple petitioners filed PILs challenging a government policy mandating biometric and digital data collection for access to welfare schemes and public services. The petitioners argued that compulsory data linking and storage violated the fundamental right to privacy. The government defended the policy as necessary for efficient service delivery and prevention of fraud. The dispute centers on whether such data collection meets constitutional standards of legality, necessity, and proportionality.

characters.name: Justice A. K. Verma
characters.details: Presiding judge hearing the constitutional bench matter

characters.name: Anil Kumar
characters.details: Social activist challenging mandatory data collection policy

characters.name: Meera Joshi
characters.details: Citizen denied welfare benefits due to non-linking of biometric data

characters.name: Rohit Bansal
characters.details: Argues necessity and public interest behind the policy

characters.name: Dr. Kavita Rao
characters.details: Data privacy expert analyzing risks of mass data collection

evidence: Government notification mandating biometric data linking
Policy documents outlining data collection and usage
Records of denial of services due to non-compliance
Technical reports on data storage and surveillance risks
Government statistics on fraud prevention through the policy
evidence_chain: Government introduces mandatory data collection policy
Citizens required to link biometric data for services
Non-compliance leads to denial of benefits
Petitioners challenge policy as violation of privacy
Government justifies policy on efficiency and fraud prevention
Court applies proportionality test (legality, necessity, balance)
Outcome depends on whether safeguards adequately protect privacy
legal_ambiguity: Whether mandatory biometric data collection and linkage is a proportionate restriction on the right to privacy or an excessive intrusion lacking adequate safeguards.
